quotes.quote: If the only tool you have is an analogy to a hammer, every problem looks like a story about nails.
quotes.summary: Muratori draws an extended analogy between modern printing presses and GPUs, inspired by his prepress work on the book Meow the Infinite. He maps GPU concepts like frame rates, multi-channel rendering, texture caches, pipeline stalls, and debug instrumentation onto their printing press equivalents. The post is deliberately impractical — a self-aware Buzzfeed-style listicle meant purely to convey enthusiasm for how impressive industrial printing technology is. He notes that web presses have fixed 'frame rates' measured in feet per second, use plate-based 'texture caches' that cause pipeline stalls on swap, and employ high-speed cameras as debug instrumentation.
quotes.key_insight: Modern industrial printing presses are remarkably analogous to GPUs in their architecture — from fixed-rate pipelines and multi-channel rendering to texture caches, pipeline stalls, and debug instrumentation — revealing that the fundamental engineering challenges of high-throughput image production transcend the digital/physical divide.

quotes.quote: Fonts that are jittery by design often look too repetitive, since the same letter sequences will tend to look the same, so it's usually better to add jitter than to build it into the font anyway.
quotes.summary: Muratori introduces Prime Pussify, a Photoshop script he created for the comic Meow the Infinite that automatically jitters font attributes on a per-character basis. The script solves the problem that Photoshop's built-in text tools are too limited to easily randomize character properties like baseline, size, and spacing. He argues that adding jitter programmatically is superior to using fonts with built-in jitter, since those tend to look repetitive with identical letter sequences. The post serves as a practical tool announcement with usage instructions and a note about Photoshop's scripting limitations.
quotes.key_insight: When creative tools lack the automation you need, writing a simple script to randomize per-character attributes produces better results than relying on pre-built jittery fonts or tedious manual adjustments.

quotes.quote: Spoiler alert: the answer is "probably not" and "definitely, here it is".
quotes.summary: In this second part of a two-part series, Muratori refines his approach to finding 'infinitely stable' half-pixel filters — filters that can be applied repeatedly without degrading the image. He builds a proper test harness that corrects for pixel shift, enabling automated convergence testing, and confirms that standard codec filters (h.264, HEVC, Lanczos) all 'explode' while his original integer filter remains stable. He then relaxes the original constraint of small-integer-only coefficients and discovers superior floating-point filters — first a 6-tap version that's nearly indistinguishable from the original image, then a remarkable 8-tap version that converges faster and preserves even more detail. He closes by noting that eigenvalue analysis can explain why these filters work, pointing readers to Fabian Giesen's detailed mathematical treatment.
quotes.key_insight: By searching the space between a known-stable integer filter and sharper but unstable Lanczos filters, you can find floating-point filter coefficients that are both infinitely stable under repeated application and nearly indistinguishable from the original image.

quotes.quote: We now know, at least for a wide range of relevant test images, that this filter is actually infinitely stable, meaning that it will never produce an artifact no matter how many times you apply it.

quotes.quote: Where bilinear filtering reduces the sharpness in an image, these common filters enhance it. They are actually sharpening the image, just like the "sharpen" operation in your favorite photo editing package might do.
quotes.summary: Muratori describes designing a 'stable' half-pixel filter for video compression that avoids the feedback artifacts produced by standard filters like those in h.264 and HEVC. Standard half-pel filters used in video codecs either blur the image (bilinear) or introduce sharpening artifacts that compound destructively when applied repeatedly across frames. He discovered that existing filters in major codecs create feedback loops where sharpening artifacts magnify with each application, eventually destroying the image. With no prior filter design experience, he found coefficients that sit on the boundary between sharpening and blurring, converging to a stable image that doesn't degrade further even after a million applications. He notes this 'stable filtering' concept appears absent from video compression literature despite seeming obviously important.
quotes.key_insight: Standard video codec half-pel filters contain hidden sharpening that creates destructive feedback loops under repeated application, but a filter tuned to the boundary between sharpening and blurring can converge to a stable image that never degrades further.

quotes.quote: Because this sharpening is procedural and based on the differences between pixels, it creates a feedback loop that will continue to sharpen the same edge over and over and over until it destroys the image.

quotes.quote: In a sane world, clang++ would have a command line switch that worked like this: clang++ --linkexe="w:/apps/msvc/x64/link.exe" test.cpp
quotes.summary: Muratori documents the absurd workaround required to make clang++ on Windows find Microsoft's link.exe linker. Rather than providing a simple command-line switch to specify the linker path, clang has ~1500 lines of code dedicated to 'finding' the linker by detecting Visual Studio installations. The only reliable solution is to reverse-engineer that detection code and construct a fake Visual Studio install directory structure. Muratori provides the minimal steps: create a fake bin/HostX64/x64 directory, copy link.exe into it, set VCToolsInstallDir, and add the real linker path to PATH for DLL resolution. He wraps everything in a batch file with setlocal to avoid environment contamination.
quotes.key_insight: Clang's auto-detection of Visual Studio installations replaces a trivial command-line flag with 1500 lines of brittle heuristics, forcing users to reverse-engineer the detection logic and build fake directory structures just to specify a linker path.

quotes.quote: The debugger in Visual Studio 2017 is a mess.
quotes.summary: Muratori evaluates whether WinDBG Preview has improved enough to replace Visual Studio as his everyday debugger. He documents Visual Studio's steady decline since version 6, noting its painfully slow watch window, inability to show out-of-scope variables, and bloated installation process. After testing WinDBG Preview, he concludes it is now viable as a daily debugger, which he considers great news for developers suffering under Visual Studio's deterioration. He provides a detailed list of remaining usability issues — keyboard shortcuts, breakpoint management, register views, and configuration handling — as constructive feedback to Microsoft. The post serves as both a practical review and a plea for Microsoft to invest in polishing WinDBG's usability to make it a definitive Visual Studio replacement.
quotes.key_insight: WinDBG Preview has finally crossed the usability threshold for daily debugging, and with modest UX polish it could fully replace Visual Studio's increasingly degraded debugger.

quotes.quote: While features and variety have improved, trust and reliability has, if anything, hit an all-time low.
quotes.summary: Muratori argues that while computing hardware has improved dramatically since 1990, software reliability has actually deteriorated. He identifies the lack of platform competition—duopolies on both desktop and phone—as a key driver, noting that OS vendors no longer treat operating systems as products but as vehicles for other business goals. The complexity of interfacing with consumer hardware creates an insurmountable barrier to new OS entrants, which also explains Linux's struggles on the desktop. He proposes that standardizing on stable instruction set architectures (ISAs) could be a first step toward fixing what he calls 'The Thirty Million Line Problem'—the overwhelming complexity that undermines software quality.
quotes.key_insight: The complexity of modern hardware interfaces has created an unbreakable OS duopoly that prioritizes business goals over reliability, and only simplifying hardware abstraction through stable ISAs can break the cycle.

quotes.quote: As the sole representative of the game industry at the conference, for the lecture topic they requested that I look back at my programming career and discuss how research either did or did not play a significant role in inspiring interesting game technology work.
quotes.summary: Muratori describes his 2016 talk at the inaugural Papers We Love conference, where he was invited as the sole game industry representative. He discussed how academic research papers influenced his game technology work, selecting Marching Cubes, Quaternions, and the Gilbert-Johnson-Keerthi distance algorithm as his focal papers. He reveals that Marching Cubes played an integral role in the robust player movement code he developed for The Witness, though he hasn't yet given a detailed talk explaining how.
quotes.key_insight: Academic research papers, even from seemingly unrelated domains like volumetric rendering, can directly inspire creative solutions to practical game development problems like player movement.

quotes.quote: Compilers only care about registers because they have to.
quotes.summary: Muratori questions why modern CPUs still use register-based instruction encodings when compilers and processors have both evolved past needing them literally. He argues that compilers naturally think in terms of memory operations and only deal with registers as an imposed constraint, while modern CPUs internally treat registers as abstract logical values anyway through out-of-order execution and register renaming. He proposes a stack-machine encoding where the top of the stack serves as a virtual register file, which could simplify context switching to saving just 3-4 values and remove artificial obfuscation between compiler and CPU. He acknowledges he may be missing something fundamental about hardware design and genuinely solicits answers from hardware engineers.
quotes.key_insight: The register abstraction in modern CPUs may be an anachronistic encoding convention that adds complexity for both compilers and processors, since both sides already reason about values abstractly rather than in terms of physical register locations.

quotes.quote: By adding the constraint that most operations have to go through registers to happen, the current paradigm effectively adds a bunch of obfuscation from the compiler to the CPU, making it harder for the CPU to tell what is actually necessary.

quotes.quote: Patents are not about protecting intellectual property from theft — that is an almost negligible part of the law.
quotes.summary: Muratori argues that all patents, but especially software patents, are fundamentally bad law with no empirical support for their existence. He explains that patents are not about preventing theft but about forcing people to pay for ideas even when independently conceived. Through a detailed email originally written to a Stanford Law student, he lays out six arguments against software patents: they're unusable in practice due to sheer volume, copyright already handles software licensing effectively, trivial concepts get patented while important ones don't, no one can define 'patent quality,' the pre-patent era was more innovative, and software patents raise First Amendment concerns. He concludes that after years of research and reading every Supreme Court case on the subject, he has found no evidence software patents are useful.
quotes.key_insight: Software patents don't protect innovation — they create an uncertainty settlement market that taxes independent invention, while all the foundational software innovations were created without them.

quotes.key_insight: Software patents don't protect innovation — they create an uncertainty settlement market that taxes independent invention, while all the foundational software innovations were created without them.

quotes.quote: He then proceeded to explain how both GJK and the expanding polytope algorithm for convex hulls were both very easy to understand when thought of geometrically.
quotes.summary: Muratori recounts how he came to implement an optimized version of the GJK intersection testing algorithm after a lunch conversation with Valve physicist Jay Stelly in 2004. Stelly explained that GJK papers were needlessly obtuse and that the algorithm was intuitive when understood geometrically rather than algebraically. Muratori implemented a solver based on this geometric insight and discovered that the required tests were much simpler than those in academic papers. He dubbed the optimized formulation 'GJKSM' and later presented it in video form and at the 2016 Papers We Love conference.
quotes.key_insight: Thinking about algorithms geometrically rather than algebraically can reveal dramatically simpler implementations that academic papers miss by being trapped in their own formalism.

quotes.quote: As everyone 'knew' at the time, you could not treat them simply as 4D vectors that could be polynomially combined.
quotes.summary: Muratori recounts his 2001 investigation into quaternion interpolation methods for the Granny 3D animation engine. Through building a visualization tool comparing linear, spherical linear, and exponential map interpolation, he discovered that linear and spherical interpolation of quaternions produce nearly identical paths, contrary to conventional wisdom. The half-angle construction of quaternions makes their linear interpolation speeds much closer to spherical interpolation than expected. This insight led him to treat quaternions as simple 4D vectors for polynomial combination, enabling order-independent animation compositing that was both faster and more flexible. He also discovered a technique exploiting quaternion double-cover (distinguishing 360 vs 720 degree rotations) to improve joint behavior during animation blending.
quotes.key_insight: Quaternion linear interpolation is far closer to spherical interpolation than conventionally believed, and this insight enables treating quaternions as simple 4D vectors for fast, order-independent animation compositing.

quotes.quote: I began to realize that 'retained' GUI code design has all the same drawbacks that typically cause us to avoid using retained-mode APIs in graphics.
quotes.summary: Casey Muratori recounts the origin of Immediate-Mode Graphical User Interfaces (IMGUI), which he developed in 2002 while building a viewer utility for Granny 3D. He realized that retained-mode GUI design suffers from the same drawbacks as retained-mode graphics APIs, and found that an immediate-mode approach was simpler, more powerful, and handled 2D/3D interaction seamlessly. He coined the term 'Single-path Immediate Mode Graphical User Interface' and shared a rough public video in 2005. He notes the technique has since gained widespread adoption, even in web development, but expresses frustration that people argue about IMGUI without truly understanding it.
quotes.key_insight: Retained-mode GUI design suffers from the same fundamental problems as retained-mode graphics, and adopting an immediate-mode approach yields simpler, more powerful interfaces — a concept so simple that people routinely overcomplicate their understanding of it.

quotes.quote: I experimented with having the GUI code operate in a more immediate fashion, like an immediate-mode graphics API, and found that everything was simpler, more powerful, and could span the gamut of both 2D and 3D interaction seamlessly.

quotes.quote: With the benefit of hindsight, I think it's fair to say that this implicit behavior was always a bad idea, it just never really caused that much of a problem because most early OpenGL usage involved a single thread targeting a single window on a single monitor.
quotes.summary: Muratori identifies a fundamental design flaw in Windows OpenGL: the implicit coupling between OpenGL contexts and Windows device contexts, which makes multi-monitor and multi-window rendering unnecessarily painful. The original API design assumed single-thread, single-window usage, but modern applications need to target multiple windows across multiple monitors. The current workaround of calling wglMakeCurrent() repeatedly is both expensive and platform-dependent, breaking the otherwise cross-platform nature of OpenGL code. Muratori proposes a simple two-function extension (wglAcquireDCFramebuffer/wglReleaseDCFramebuffer) that leverages OpenGL's existing framebuffer API to make output targeting explicit rather than implicit. He also addresses the threading caveat by requiring fence synchronization when multiple contexts target the same framebuffer, keeping the driver simple.
quotes.key_insight: Implicit API couplings that seem harmless in simple use cases become architectural liabilities at scale, and the fix is often to make the implicit behavior explicit using mechanisms the API already provides.

quotes.quote: wglMakeCurrent() is not assumed to be a lightweight operation on OpenGL drivers, and in general the OpenGL driver has no way of knowing a priori that all you're doing with the wglMakeCurrent() is flip-flopping between two consistently targeted windows.

quotes.quote: I don't think people who make APIs really appreciate how important it is to get them right, and how much unnecessary work their mistakes can cause for hundreds, thousands, sometimes even millions of other programmers.
quotes.summary: Muratori dissects the Event Tracing for Windows API as a case study in catastrophically bad API design. He first demonstrates what a clean API for the same task would look like — a few straightforward function calls to receive context switch events — then walks through the actual Microsoft implementation step by agonizing step. The real API requires massive grab-bag structures with undocumented magic numbers, manual memory arithmetic for string packing, elevated administrator privileges, and — most absurdly — a dedicated thread that blocks forever just to receive events via callback. The core argument is that writing usage code first would have prevented all of these design failures, since the underlying problem is trivially simple: copying structured data from kernel to user space.
quotes.key_insight: Writing usage code first — imagining the ideal API from the caller's perspective before considering implementation — is the single most effective practice for preventing bad API design, because it immediately exposes unnecessary complexity.

quotes.quote: To the best of my knowledge, it was the first (and last?) time anyone enumerated concrete, specific characteristics of APIs that could be directly observed and evaluated to determine whether an API would be effective in practice.
quotes.summary: Muratori introduces a 2004 lecture on API design that he considers a landmark in establishing concrete, evaluable characteristics for reusable components. He recounts how the prevailing wisdom in game development held that only 'leaf technologies' (isolated, non-interdependent libraries) could succeed as middleware, and how Granny 3D's 1.x branch initially confirmed this belief. After a year of redesigning the API for the 2.x branch with Jeff Roberts, they cracked the problem of making deeply integrated systems reusable, shipping in over 2600 SKUs. The lecture codified five key characteristics of effective APIs, including the concept of 'integration discontinuity' as the primary pitfall to avoid.
quotes.key_insight: Reusable APIs for deeply integrated systems can succeed where conventional wisdom says they cannot, but only if designed around concrete, evaluable characteristics that prevent integration discontinuities.

quotes.quote: This is also editor-only code, which means it is not meant to be used in a released product, and thus the emphasis is entirely on keeping implementation costs low, and not on ancillary things like robustness that might be more of a priority in code meant for end users.
quotes.summary: Casey Muratori shares the complete ~2000 line source code for the Lister Panel from The Witness's level editor, inviting readers to examine it and submit questions for a Q&A session. He notes the code is a mixture of his and Jon Blow's coding conventions, adopted for maintainability. The code implements a sophisticated entity filtering, listing, and visualization system for the game's editor. Muratori emphasizes this is editor-only code where implementation cost is prioritized over robustness, since it won't ship to end users. The post serves as a rare window into production game development tooling on a major indie title.
quotes.key_insight: Editor and tooling code for games should prioritize low implementation cost and developer productivity over the robustness standards applied to shipping code, because the audience and failure consequences are fundamentally different.

quotes.quote: The problem with the internet is that everyone thinks they're an expert. That would be fine, if everyone actually was an expert, but the truth is that only I am an expert, so it's just frustrating.
quotes.summary: Muratori describes implementing hierarchical group display in The Witness editor's Lister Panel, which previously only supported flat entity lists. He walks through the design decisions involved in recursive tree traversal, where to store expansion state, and how to implement filtering that preserves hierarchy. The post covers practical considerations like stack space in recursive functions, why UI state shouldn't live on domain objects, and an efficient algorithm for pulling parent groups into filtered views. Along the way, he shares a real war story about a recursive function that overflowed the stack in debug mode. The technical content is wrapped in an elaborate running joke about Chronicles of Narnia references.
quotes.key_insight: Practical UI programming requires careful decisions about where state lives and how traversal algorithms handle filtering — keeping UI state out of domain objects and using efficient ancestor-walking algorithms avoids both architectural bloat and performance pitfalls.

quotes.summary: Muratori describes implementing special filters for The Witness's Lister Panel editor UI. He walks through the design process of creating a table-driven filtering system that handles three-state results (pass, fail, not applicable), then extends it with parametric filters using an integer parameter to avoid writing redundant per-flag filter functions. He further piggybacks editing capability onto the same filter functions, allowing users to both filter and modify entity properties through the same code path, culminating in bulk editing across all listed entities.
quotes.key_insight: By designing filter functions that both query and modify entity properties through the same code path, you can build a flexible editor UI with filtering, per-entity editing, and bulk editing from very little code.

quotes.quote: A lot of programmers — including myself, more times than I care to admit — end up plowing through these situations by just guessing at what the interface should be and then moving on.
quotes.summary: Casey Muratori walks through implementing the type filtering system for the Lister Panel in The Witness's editor tools. He starts with a simple update function that loops over all entities and checks them against a filter, then builds out the filter using a fixed-size boolean array indexed by entity type. He explains his decision to use a fixed array over a dynamic one due to unfamiliarity with The Witness codebase's utility classes, and demonstrates his practice of writing usage code before finalizing an API. The post illustrates compression-oriented programming by showing how a single set_all_types_to helper function composes with direct array access to cover every needed operation.
quotes.key_insight: Writing out concrete usage code before designing an API reveals the right abstraction naturally and prevents over-engineering, especially in unfamiliar codebases where simpler constructs reduce risk.

He explains his decision to use a fixed array over a dynamic one due to unfamiliarity with The Witness codebase's utility classes, and demonstrates his practice of writing usage code before finalizing an API. The post illustrates compression-oriented programming by showing how a single set_all_types_to helper function composes with direct array access to cover every needed operation.
quotes.key_insight: Writing out concrete usage code before designing an API reveals the right abstraction naturally and prevents over-engineering, especially in unfamiliar codebases where simpler constructs reduce risk.

quotes.quote: The sad truth is that most of the time, if you think you are going to replace something simple like an enum that's used to control a mode with a class hierarchy where you have one class per mode, you often end up implementing the enum scheme inside it anyway.
quotes.summary: Muratori examines the seemingly trivial decision of defining a C++ enum for a UI panel mode in The Witness editor, using it as a springboard to critique object-oriented class hierarchies. He argues that a simple enum with a count sentinel provides zero-initialization, easy iteration, trivial serialization, and flexible code organization — all of which a class hierarchy would make harder. He demonstrates that OOP's code segregation into separate files and virtual functions creates O(n) maintenance costs for common changes like adding parameters, while providing O(1) only for changes to individual object internals — which are actually the least frequent modifications. The post concludes that the sheer depth of reasoning behind even seven lines of code illustrates why programming well is genuinely hard.
quotes.key_insight: Simple enums with zero-default conventions provide more flexibility, less typing, and better maintainability than class hierarchies, which impose O(n) costs on the most common code changes while optimizing for the least frequent ones.

quotes.quote: This kind of code segregation often goes by positive-sounding names, depending on the particulars, such as 'abstraction', 'encapsulation', 'division of responsibility', etc. But what it really should be called is 'obfuscation', because that's the one thing that it definitely always does.

quotes.quote: It's OK to accept a solution that's a little ugly if the alternatives are uglier.
quotes.summary: Muratori continues his series on compression-oriented programming by demonstrating how to progressively compress UI code from The Witness editor while maintaining 'continuous granularity' — ensuring that higher-level convenience functions never eliminate access to lower-level ones. He shows how naively modifying a push_button() function to handle toggle booleans would create a 'granularity discontinuity' that makes intermediate use cases awkward, and instead advocates adding a new bool_button() layer on top. He argues that programmers should resist the temptation to over-engineer solutions to language limitations, focusing on total cost rather than abstract notions of code cleanliness. He also clarifies that his critique of object-oriented programming targets the practice of orienting design around objects, not the incidental existence of object-like structures, and pushes back on claims that compression-oriented programming doesn't scale to teams.
quotes.key_insight: Good API design requires continuous granularity — building higher-level convenience functions as layers on top of lower-level ones rather than replacing them, so users are never forced to jump between extreme abstraction levels to accomplish intermediate tasks.

quotes.quote: I like to think of efficiency as holistically as possible. If you look at the development process for a piece of code as a whole, you won't overlook any hidden costs.
quotes.summary: Muratori advocates for 'semantic compression' as a programming methodology, where code is written straightforwardly first, then iteratively compressed by extracting shared patterns into reusable structures and functions — similar to how a dictionary compressor finds redundancy. He demonstrates this through a real example from The Witness's editor UI code, showing step-by-step how repetitive layout and button code was compressed into a clean Panel_Layout abstraction. He argues this bottom-up approach is vastly superior to object-oriented methodologies that try to design class hierarchies before writing any working code, because starting with concrete details and compressing upward naturally produces well-fitting, easy-to-use abstractions.
quotes.key_insight: Good abstractions emerge naturally from compressing working code that has real duplication, not from designing class hierarchies before any code exists.

quotes.quote: Most programmers don't know much about randomness beyond how to call the random number generator in the standard library of their favorite programming language.
quotes.summary: Casey Muratori explains the concept of colored noise (white, blue, red, green) and its practical importance in procedural content placement, specifically in The Witness's grass planting system. He traces his own education from a 2005 conversation with rendering researcher Don Mitchell, who introduced him to blue noise patterns. The post dissects how The Witness's grass planter used naive Poisson disk sampling, which suffers from catastrophic computational costs as valid placement regions shrink, leading to visible gaps. He then describes a wavefront-based directed sampling approach (similar to Bridson's algorithm) that dramatically improves efficiency by only searching neighborhoods of existing points rather than the entire space. The post promises a follow-up addressing a subtler theoretical problem with the algorithm.
quotes.key_insight: Naive random sampling becomes catastrophically inefficient as valid regions shrink; directing samples to the frontier of already-placed points transforms an intractable problem into a tractable one, a principle that applies far beyond graphics.

He traces his own education from a 2005 conversation with rendering researcher Don Mitchell, who introduced him to blue noise patterns. The post dissects how The Witness's grass planter used naive Poisson disk sampling, which suffers from catastrophic computational costs as valid placement regions shrink, leading to visible gaps. He then describes a wavefront-based directed sampling approach (similar to Bridson's algorithm) that dramatically improves efficiency by only searching neighborhoods of existing points rather than the entire space. The post promises a follow-up addressing a subtler theoretical problem with the algorithm.
quotes.key_insight: Naive random sampling becomes catastrophically inefficient as valid regions shrink; directing samples to the frontier of already-placed points transforms an intractable problem into a tractable one, a principle that applies far beyond graphics.

quotes.quote: Your ratio of meaningful to meaningless samples is literally the measure of how effective your algorithm is at producing the walkable area.

quotes.quote: There's too much stuff to talk about, even if I leave a lot out, and it's too hard to emphasize the important parts when I can't easily point at parts of diagrams.
quotes.summary: Muratori wraps up his grass rendering series by introducing the mathematical concept of interpolation through an experimental video format rather than text. He found that text was inadequate for explaining the visual and diagrammatic nature of interpolation, so he recorded himself drawing on a notebook. The video covers linear blending, barycentric coordinates, and Bezier curves, though he ran out of time to cover bilinear interpolation and higher-degree Bezier constructions. He reflects positively on the video tutorial format and teases the next Witness Wednesdays topic: a case study on building a feature-rich editor panel with minimal code.
quotes.key_insight: The core mathematical concept of linear blending (interpolation) is a single equation that appears everywhere in graphics — from Bezier curves to bilinear texture sampling to barycentric coordinates — and understanding this unifying pattern is more valuable than memorizing each application separately.

quotes.quote: When humans want something 'randomly' scattered, what they really expect to see is actually a very specific distribution that maintains a relatively constant density.
quotes.summary: Muratori describes his work fixing the grass planting system in The Witness ahead of the PS4 unveiling trailer. When artists complained about 'huge gaps' in scattered geometry, he first added debug visualization rather than diving into the algorithm code. This revealed that the fern meshes were off-center in their source art, causing gaps that had nothing to do with the distribution algorithm. After fixing the mesh centering, smaller gaps remained, leading him to investigate the actual scattering algorithm. The post illustrates how debug visualization tools can quickly expose the real cause of visual problems before any algorithmic work begins.
quotes.key_insight: Debug visualization should be the first step in diagnosing visual problems, because making intermediate state visible often reveals that the real cause is entirely different from what everyone assumed.

quotes.quote: In my mind, one of the most important things on any project is minimizing the amount of time it takes for a change to be testable. Ideally, it should take less than ten seconds for a programmer to go from a changed source file to a running executable.
quotes.summary: Muratori describes several technical features he built for The Witness's grass planting system, including variable-distance blue noise distribution, obstacle avoidance via raycasting, and edge-distance control using signed distance fields. He created a minimal test world called 'CaseyTown' to achieve fast iteration times, since the full game's asset streaming made startup slow. Despite building the system under deadline pressure for the PS4 announcement trailer, none of his grass improvements were actually needed for the trailer — though artists later adopted the features for production. The post illustrates how real production use exposed gaps in the initial feature set, particularly around controlling grass behavior at obstacle boundaries.
quotes.key_insight: Practical game development solutions are often pragmatic rather than elegant — planting grass twice to discover boundaries, accepting imperfect raycasts, and building features that miss their original deadline but prove valuable later.

quotes.quote: Better yet, if one were a super duper programmer — like, we're talking healthcare.gov-level programmers here — you could even change the for loops to properly compute the starting and ending radii from the first intersection with the sample region.
quotes.summary: Muratori follows up on his previous article about staggered concentric intersection patterns for grass placement, responding to reader questions. He provides the actual code used to generate the pattern, addresses scalability concerns about extending the pattern to larger areas, and explores potential approaches for making it tile infinitely. He also demonstrates that the scalloping artifacts are nearly invisible when rendered against a realistic ground texture rather than a high-contrast white background, and investigates Fermat spirals as an alternative pattern. He concludes by clarifying that this is not a grass simulation but an area-coverage problem.
quotes.key_insight: The staggered concentric intersection pattern for grass placement is more robust than expected at high densities and its artifacts are nearly invisible against realistic ground textures, though extending it to infinite coverage remains an open problem requiring either periodic regularization, alternative curves, or tiling with seam fixups.

quotes.quote: If there's one thing I can say has been true throughout my entire programming career, it's that it has always paid to ask why I was doing things the way I was doing them.
quotes.summary: Muratori describes his quest to solve what he calls 'The Nebraska Problem' — visible linear gaps that appear in blue noise patterns used for grass placement in The Witness. After multiple failed attempts using modified blue noise, gap targeting, relaxation, and directional constraints, he realizes that randomness itself is the obstacle: no random scheme can produce optimal coverage. He ultimately solves the problem with a deterministic pattern based on staggered concentric circle intersections, which provides better visual coverage with fewer points than blue noise. The key insight is that when optimality is the goal, you may need to abandon randomness entirely in favor of ordered, geometric approaches.
quotes.key_insight: When optimizing for a specific criterion, randomness is inherently limiting — sometimes you must abandon stochastic approaches entirely and drive at the optimum with deterministic, geometric solutions.

He ultimately solves the problem with a deterministic pattern based on staggered concentric circle intersections, which provides better visual coverage with fewer points than blue noise. The key insight is that when optimality is the goal, you may need to abandon randomness entirely in favor of ordered, geometric approaches.
quotes.key_insight: When optimizing for a specific criterion, randomness is inherently limiting — sometimes you must abandon stochastic approaches entirely and drive at the optimum with deterministic, geometric solutions.

quotes.quote: Sometimes, when there's a lot to do, you'll live with a bug fix because it works, rather than because it makes sense.
quotes.summary: Muratori recounts debugging a transform manipulator bug in The Witness that he initially misdiagnosed as a floating point precision issue. After implementing a fix that worked but didn't fully make sense to him, he spent his Christmas break obsessing over why. Upon returning and stepping through the code, he discovered the real culprit: the plane library's get_normal() function silently normalized the a,b,c values, making them inconsistent with the d value computed during plane construction. The bug was fundamentally about mismatched assumptions between two programmers' mental models of what a 'plane' object means.
quotes.key_insight: A bug fix that works for the wrong reasons is a ticking time bomb — the real virtue of a programmer is the compulsion to understand why a fix works, not just that it does.

quotes.quote: Working in someone else's codebase is much like moving to a foreign country that happens to use the same alphabet as you do, but not the same language: technically, you can read, but you don't really have any idea what most things mean.
quotes.summary: Muratori recounts debugging a mysterious bug in The Witness's editor where a translation manipulator would fail to move objects along certain axes depending on camera angle. After methodically ruling out plane construction errors and axis-specific issues, he discovered the problem lay in the ray-versus-plane intersection function — specifically that it doesn't check whether the intersection occurs in front of or behind the camera. The post walks through the geometric reasoning behind translation manipulators, the debugging process, and ends as a cliffhanger challenge for readers to identify the bug themselves.
quotes.key_insight: A ray-plane intersection that doesn't reject negative t values (intersections behind the camera) will produce erratic, camera-angle-dependent behavior that manifests as objects refusing to move along certain axes — a classic 3D math pitfall hidden inside the simplest possible geometric operations.

quotes.quote: The great thing about programming on Windows is that it is the only commercially viable platform where you can ship software to users without getting approval from a giant bureaucracy.
quotes.summary: Muratori chronicles debugging a five-second UI stall in The Witness caused by a low-level keyboard hook (SetWindowsHookEx) that blocked input processing during game loading. The hook was Microsoft's own recommended workaround for disabling Windows logo keys in fullscreen games, but it conflicted with Raw Input, which the game needed for mouse movement. After hours of experimentation, the fix turned out to be a single undocumented flag (RIDEV_NOHOTKEYS) during Raw Input initialization. Muratori uses this experience to argue that poorly designed platform APIs waste enormous aggregate developer time across the industry, turning what should be simple operations into hours of archaeology through broken documentation and conflicting subsystems.
quotes.key_insight: Poorly designed platform APIs impose a massive hidden tax on the entire software industry, as every team must independently spend hours debugging the same preventable problems that could be eliminated by investing in clear, well-designed interfaces.

quotes.quote: The last thing you ever want to happen is to have the player notice the fact that they're sitting at a computer asking it to move a virtual viewpoint around.
quotes.summary: Casey Muratori describes building 'Walk Monster,' a tool for testing and visualizing player movement code in The Witness. Starting from the need to bulletproof player collision so it never breaks immersion, he iterates through several spatial exploration algorithms — from Rapidly Exploring Random Trees to a custom flood fill to his final 'Localized Directional Sampling' approach — to map everywhere the player can go. Each algorithm reveals tradeoffs between sampling density, boundary accuracy, and computational efficiency. The tool immediately finds real bugs including walkable slopes that shouldn't be, phantom collision volumes, and one-way-traversible rocks. He concludes by outlining open questions about boundary probing, visualization, and plans to fix the movement code itself.
quotes.key_insight: Effective testing requires exercising the actual system under test, not proxies for it, and algorithm selection must match the actual dimensionality and goals of your problem rather than the superficial shape of it.

quotes.quote: This vision of a future Windows heavily censored by Microsoft is chilling. But how likely is it to actually occur?
quotes.summary: Muratori argues that Windows 8's closed app distribution model poses a serious long-term threat to software innovation. By requiring all modern UI apps to go through the Windows Store, Microsoft is establishing itself as sole gatekeeper for what software can run on Windows — a position that will become increasingly dangerous as the desktop interface is inevitably phased out. Drawing a direct parallel to the DOS-to-Windows transition of the 1990s, he predicts the classic desktop will be obsolete within 10-20 years, leaving users trapped in a censored ecosystem where today's most acclaimed games and culturally significant software would already be banned under current certification rules. He methodically demonstrates that Microsoft has almost no financial incentive to keep distribution closed, since they already allow third-party payment processing within apps. The piece concludes that Microsoft should open Windows 8's distribution model to differentiate from Apple's closed iOS and attract the developer support critical to competing in the tablet and phone markets.
quotes.key_insight: A platform owner who controls software distribution must be able to perfectly predict the future of computing to avoid accidentally prohibiting innovation — since no company can do this, the only rational policy is open distribution.

quotes.summary: Muratori argues that Windows 8's closed app distribution model poses a serious long-term threat to software innovation. By requiring all modern UI apps to go through the Windows Store, Microsoft is establishing itself as sole gatekeeper for what software can run on Windows — a position that will become increasingly dangerous as the desktop interface is inevitably phased out. Drawing a direct parallel to the DOS-to-Windows transition of the 1990s, he predicts the classic desktop will be obsolete within 10-20 years, leaving users trapped in a censored ecosystem where today's most acclaimed games and culturally significant software would already be banned under current certification rules. He methodically demonstrates that Microsoft has almost no financial incentive to keep distribution closed, since they already allow third-party payment processing within apps. The piece concludes that Microsoft should open Windows 8's distribution model to differentiate from Apple's closed iOS and attract the developer support critical to competing in the tablet and phone markets.
quotes.key_insight: A platform owner who controls software distribution must be able to perfectly predict the future of computing to avoid accidentally prohibiting innovation — since no company can do this, the only rational policy is open distribution.
